disease.name: Gilbert Syndrome
disease.note: Benign autosomal recessive condition causing unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia due to reduced UGT1A1 enzyme activity (30% of normal). Affects 5-10% of population. Episodic jaundice triggered by fasting, stress, illness, or exertion. No treatment needed; excellent prognosis.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Gilbert syndrome is universally benign; no emergency required. Elevated conjugated bilirubin or abnormal LFTs suggest alternative diagnosis.

historyFeatures.note: Mild, often first noticed in adolescence or young adulthood
Often coincidental; Gilbert syndrome itself does not cause significant fatigue
Classic trigger: skipping meals, fasting, dehydration
Intercurrent illness, infection, or surgery can unmask jaundice
Strenuous exertion can precipitate mild jaundice
Autosomal recessive; family members may have history of benign jaundice
Not typical in Gilbert; if present, consider alternative diagnosis

examFeatures.note: Often mild and fluctuates; may only be visible in natural light
Not typical; consider hemolysis if present

subtypes.name: Classic Gilbert Syndrome
subtypes.note: TA7/TA7 homozygous promoter mutation. Bilirubin 1-5 mg/dL, predominantly unconjugated. Normal LFTs and hemolysis markers. Benign course.

laboratory.name: Total and direct bilirubin
laboratory.indication: elevated total bilirubin (1-5 mg/dL), direct fraction <20%; hallmark of Gilbert

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT)
laboratory.indication: normal in Gilbert syndrome; any elevation suggests alternative diagnosis

laboratory.name: FBC with reticulocyte count
laboratory.indication: normal in Gilbert; reticulocytosis suggests hemolysis

laboratory.name: LDH and haptoglobin
laboratory.indication: normal in Gilbert; elevated LDH and low haptoglobin suggest hemolysis

laboratory.name: INR/PT
laboratory.indication: normal in Gilbert; prolonged PT suggests liver disease

laboratory.name: UGT1A1 genotyping
laboratory.indication: promoter polymorphism (TA7/TA7) confirms diagnosis; not required for typical presentations

laboratory.name: Fasting test (caloric restriction)
laboratory.indication: 24-48h fasting increases bilirubin 2-3x in Gilbert; rarely needed for diagnosis

management.protocolReference: EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Management of Gilbert Syndrome; AASLD Guidance

pathways.criteria: Confirmed Gilbert syndrome, any bilirubin level
pathways.treatment: Reassure patient that Gilbert syndrome is benign and requires no treatment. Avoid prolonged fasting (eat regular meals). Stay hydrated. No activity restrictions. No medications needed. Avoid unnecessary investigations for mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Inform patient that jaundice may become more noticeable during illness, fasting, or stress — this is expected and harmless.
